meta.title: Creative Mathematics Learning — Wisdom → Conceptual Constraints → Constraint Movement → Topic Architecture → Learning Design → Agility Evidence → Cases
meta.subtitle: A constraint lattice for teaching mathematics creatively: learners move between space, shape, freedom, similarity, difference, number, representation, proof, modelling and concrete cases. Mathematical understanding is treated as agility across levels, not merely procedural performance.
meta.searchPlaceholder: space, shape, freedom, constraint, similarity, difference, pattern, number, place value, fraction, ratio, symmetry, graph, function, proof, data, probability, modelling, representation, manipulatives, conjecture…
meta.userContextPlaceholder: e.g., Year 5 fractions; creative secondary algebra; mixed-attainment class; geometry outdoors; maths anxiety; need more agency, dialogue and visual reasoning…
meta.promptTemplate:
You are an expert guide to creative mathematics education with deep judgement about mathematical constraint, learner agency, representation, dialogue, proof, modelling and conceptual progression.

Write a realistic narrative case-study showing how upstream constraints (space, shape, freedom, similarity, difference, representation and agency) shape downstream learning of number, geometry, algebra, data, probability and proof.
Use the selected concepts to explain how learners move up and down the constraint lattice: from concrete experience to diagrams, symbols, generalisation, proof and back again.

User context (if provided):
{{USER_CONTEXT}}

Selected concepts:
{{SELECTED_TITLES}}

Details:
{{SELECTED_DETAILS}}

Trace paths:
{{TRACE_SUMMARY}}

Deliver:
- Scenario description (learners, topic, environment, constraints)
- Diagnosis of learning barriers (procedure lock-in, representation gaps, anxiety, false patterns, assessment pressure)
- Creative mathematics design (task, materials, representations, dialogue, variation and challenge)
- Constraint-lattice map: how learners move from high-level ideas to specific mathematical topics and back
- Classroom playbook (teacher prompts, group routines, examples, counterexamples and re-entry routes)
- Evidence plan: indicators of agility, explanation, transfer, fluency and revision
- 2 safe-to-try experiments with backfire checks

levels.title: Generative mathematical wisdom
levels.hint: Judgement-in-context: curiosity, freedom, constraint, attention, play, precision and care.

levels.title: High-level conceptual constraints
levels.hint: Space, shape, pattern, difference, similarity, transformation, measure, quantity, uncertainty and representation.

levels.title: Constraint-lattice movement
levels.hint: How learners move between concrete action, diagrams, symbols, proof, examples, counterexamples and generalisation.

levels.title: Mathematical topic architecture
levels.hint: Number, algebra, geometry, measurement, data, probability, functions, proof and modelling as linked territories.

levels.title: Learning design mechanisms
levels.hint: Tasks, manipulatives, dialogue, visualisation, embodied work, digital tools, assessment moments and classroom routines.

levels.title: Agility, evidence & improvement
levels.hint: How mathematical agility is noticed, supported, assessed and improved without reducing creativity to test scores.

levels.title: Concrete cases, tensions & pathologies
levels.hint: Classroom scenarios where constraints either open mathematical imagination or trap learners in brittle procedures.

nodes.label: Wonder before technique
nodes.summary: Mathematics begins when something invites noticing: a shadow, rhythm, tiling, balance, game or surprising regularity.
nodes.bodyHtml: Creative mathematics teaching treats wonder as a real constraint: learners need time to notice, compare, ask and dwell before formal methods are introduced.

nodes.label: Freedom within constraint
nodes.summary: Creativity grows when learners have room to choose strategies while still meeting shared mathematical constraints.
nodes.bodyHtml: Freedom is not the absence of structure. It is the capacity to explore within boundaries: same total, different shapes; same pattern, different representation; same problem, multiple routes.

nodes.label: Proportion & pacing
nodes.summary: Choose the right degree of challenge, explanation, practice and open exploration for the moment.
nodes.bodyHtml: Too much openness can bewilder; too much procedure can deaden. Wise pacing keeps learners near the edge of productive difficulty.

nodes.label: Equity of mathematical voice
nodes.summary: Treat different ways of seeing as legitimate evidence in the classroom conversation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Creative mathematics depends on more than fast answers. Gesture, drawing, story, pattern spotting and informal language can all become routes into precision.

nodes.label: Wholeness of mathematical experience
nodes.summary: Connect number, shape, space, movement, language, emotion and purpose rather than isolating topics.
nodes.bodyHtml: Mathematics becomes meaningful when learners experience relations between territories: fractions as measure, ratio as geometry, algebra as pattern, data as argument.

nodes.label: Safe-to-try mathematical experiments
nodes.summary: Let learners test conjectures, fail productively, revise and compare approaches.
nodes.bodyHtml: A creative mathematics classroom makes wrong turns discussable. Error becomes information about constraints, not evidence of incapacity.

nodes.label: Reflection & re-seeing
nodes.summary: Return to a problem from another level: concrete, visual, symbolic, verbal, general or applied.
nodes.bodyHtml: Reflection builds agility: learners learn not only a method, but how to change altitude in the constraint lattice when stuck.

nodes.label: Space, shape & orientation
nodes.summary: Mathematics as organised space: position, direction, symmetry, boundary, inside/outside and transformation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Spatial constraints let learners reason before symbols: rotating, folding, building, mapping, comparing and imagining alternatives.

nodes.label: Difference & similarity
nodes.summary: Learning mathematics means noticing what changes, what stays invariant and what counts as the same.
nodes.bodyHtml: Similarity and difference drive classification, pattern, proof, proportionality and algebraic generalisation.

nodes.label: Freedom, rule and constraint
nodes.summary: Rules can restrict, but they can also create playable mathematical worlds.
nodes.bodyHtml: A grid, a number system, a game rule or a symmetry condition can generate rich exploration when learners can vary one thing at a time.

nodes.label: Representation & translation
nodes.summary: Move between objects, diagrams, language, tables, graphs, symbols and stories.
nodes.bodyHtml: Understanding deepens when learners see the same structure through different representational lenses and can translate between them.

nodes.label: Quantity, measure & scale
nodes.summary: Numbers become meaningful through magnitude, unit, comparison, estimation and scale.
nodes.bodyHtml: Measure connects bodily experience, tools, units, proportional reasoning and later formal calculation.

nodes.label: Uncertainty, variation & distribution
nodes.summary: Not all mathematics is exact; some concerns chance, spread, evidence and expectation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Variation invites learners to reason about data, probability, fairness, sampling and uncertainty rather than only single correct answers.

nodes.label: Data → pattern → insight ladder
nodes.summary: Collecting data is not enough; learners must organise, interpret, question and communicate it.
nodes.bodyHtml: Creative mathematical work turns observations into patterns and patterns into claims that can be challenged.

nodes.label: Collaborative mathematical conversation
nodes.summary: Mathematics is strengthened by comparing methods, asking for reasons and coordinating partial insights.
nodes.bodyHtml: Peers can supply different representations, counterexamples and strategies; the classroom becomes a thinking system.

nodes.label: Concrete ↔ abstract movement
nodes.summary: Learners climb and descend between manipulatives, images, words, symbols and formal ideas.
nodes.bodyHtml: Agility means not being trapped at one level. A learner can return to a model, draw a diagram, invent notation or state a general rule.

nodes.label: Mathematical feedback loops
nodes.summary: Try → notice → adjust → explain → test again.
nodes.bodyHtml: Feedback loops make mathematics investigative. Learners see that strategy, evidence and explanation change one another.

nodes.label: Productive drift and unproductive drift
nodes.summary: Exploration can open new mathematics, but it can also lose the problem’s constraints.
nodes.bodyHtml: Teachers help learners distinguish fruitful side routes from drift that abandons the mathematical object.

nodes.label: Interfaces between representations
nodes.summary: Breakdowns occur when a drawing, table, graph, story or formula is treated as the whole idea.
nodes.bodyHtml: Many misconceptions are translation problems: learners know something in one form but cannot move it to another.

nodes.label: Boundaries, definitions & cases
nodes.summary: Definitions decide what belongs, what does not and what must be tested.
nodes.bodyHtml: Mathematics advances by sharpening boundaries: square/rectangle, prime/composite, linear/nonlinear, sample/population.

nodes.label: Flow of attention and cognitive load
nodes.summary: Attention is finite; task design must manage memory, notation, language and visual complexity.
nodes.bodyHtml: Creative tasks still need thoughtful sequencing so learners are not overloaded before they can reason.

nodes.label: Assessment, authority & mathematical agency
nodes.summary: What counts as success shapes whether learners explore or merely comply.
nodes.bodyHtml: If only speed and neatness are rewarded, learners hide experiments. If reasoning and representation are valued, agency grows.

nodes.label: Problem types: exercise / puzzle / investigation / model
nodes.summary: Different mathematical situations require different forms of teacher support.
nodes.bodyHtml: An exercise consolidates; a puzzle invites structure seeking; an investigation invites conjecture; a model connects mathematics to a situation.

nodes.label: Curriculum scope & learning journey
nodes.summary: Sequence topics as a connected landscape rather than a checklist of isolated procedures.
nodes.bodyHtml: A creative mathematics curriculum makes routes visible: number to algebra, shape to proof, data to argument, measurement to modelling.

nodes.label: Pedagogical governance
nodes.summary: Class norms, teacher moves and assessment routines protect inquiry while sustaining mathematical rigour.
nodes.bodyHtml: Governance means designing the conditions under which freedom, care, challenge and precision can coexist.

nodes.label: Classroom information ecology
nodes.summary: Boards, notebooks, manipulatives, digital tools and talk determine what mathematical information can be seen and reused.
nodes.bodyHtml: Creative learning needs shared visible traces: diagrams, conjecture lists, failed attempts, class examples and evolving definitions.

nodes.label: Topic backbone: number–shape–data–change
nodes.summary: Use a coherent mathematical backbone that links key territories across ages and contexts.
nodes.bodyHtml: The backbone helps learners locate a task: is this about quantity, spatial relation, variation, transformation or structure?

nodes.label: Integration across topics
nodes.summary: Connect mathematical domains so learners can transfer ideas across contexts.
nodes.bodyHtml: Fractions, ratio, slope, probability and scale can illuminate one another when taught as connected constraints.

nodes.label: Portfolio of task types
nodes.summary: Balance fluency tasks, open investigations, visual tasks, modelling tasks, games and proofs.
nodes.bodyHtml: A rich task portfolio prevents creativity from becoming vague and prevents fluency from becoming mechanical.

nodes.label: Anticipate–Represent–Explore–Constrain
nodes.summary: A learning cycle for creative mathematics: anticipate possibilities, represent them, explore variations, then constrain and justify.
nodes.bodyHtml: This cycle helps teachers convert playful activity into mathematical generalisation and explanation.

nodes.label: Challenge design → learning management
nodes.summary: Analyse likely stumbling points and design supports, extensions and re-entry routes.
nodes.bodyHtml: Teachers manage learning risk: confusion, boredom, false pattern, notation overload and premature closure.

nodes.label: Hierarchy of mathematical constraints
nodes.summary: Move from broad constraints such as space and sameness to precise constraints such as equality, unit, angle or function rule.
nodes.bodyHtml: The constraint hierarchy makes mathematical abstraction visible: a learner can ask which constraint is doing the work.

nodes.label: Investigation & example strategy
nodes.summary: Choose examples, non-examples, boundary cases and counterexamples deliberately.
nodes.bodyHtml: Examples are not decoration. They are the material through which learners test and refine concepts.

nodes.label: Adaptive practice strategy
nodes.summary: Practice changes according to learner insight: vary constraints, representations and contexts rather than repeat blindly.
nodes.bodyHtml: Adaptive practice builds fluency with meaning by asking: what has changed, what is invariant, and why does the method still work?

nodes.label: Number sense & place value
nodes.summary: Magnitude, unitising, place value, estimation, operations and flexible calculation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Number sense is the foundation for moving between concrete quantities, mental strategies, written algorithms and later algebra.

nodes.label: Fractions, ratio & proportion
nodes.summary: Parts, wholes, scaling, equivalence, multiplicative comparison and proportional change.
nodes.bodyHtml: This topic is a central bridge between number, measure, geometry, probability, slope and modelling.

nodes.label: Algebra, pattern & functions
nodes.summary: Variables, generalisation, expressions, equations, sequences, mappings and functional relationships.
nodes.bodyHtml: Algebra becomes creative when learners see it as a language for structure, not merely a procedure for unknowns.

nodes.label: Geometry, symmetry & transformation
nodes.summary: Shape properties, angle, symmetry, congruence, similarity, coordinates and transformations.
nodes.bodyHtml: Geometry lets learners reason through seeing, moving, constructing and proving.

nodes.label: Measurement, units & dimensional thinking
nodes.summary: Length, area, volume, time, mass, compound measures, estimation and unit conversion.
nodes.bodyHtml: Measurement connects mathematics to embodied experience and exposes the need for agreed units and precision.

nodes.label: Data, probability & uncertainty
nodes.summary: Collecting, visualising and interpreting data; chance, fairness, expectation and variation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Data and probability support reasoning in uncertain situations and challenge the idea that mathematics is always deterministic.

nodes.label: Mathematical modelling
nodes.summary: Turn situations into mathematical structures, test assumptions and interpret results back in context.
nodes.bodyHtml: Modelling is a high-level agility practice: choose constraints, simplify, calculate, interpret, revise.

nodes.label: Proof, argument & justification
nodes.summary: Explain why something must be true, not merely that it worked in examples.
nodes.bodyHtml: Proof grows from pattern, example, counterexample and the pressure to convince others.

nodes.label: Visualisation & manipulatives
nodes.summary: Use objects, drawings, dynamic geometry, arrays, bars, grids and movement to make constraints visible.
nodes.bodyHtml: Manipulatives matter when they are linked to language and symbols, not used as isolated activity.

nodes.label: Dialogue routines & mathematical talk
nodes.summary: Use talk moves, comparison of strategies, conjecture prompts and explanation stems.
nodes.bodyHtml: Mathematical talk helps learners coordinate different routes through the same constraint lattice.

nodes.label: Digital and dynamic tools
nodes.summary: Use spreadsheets, simulations, graphing tools, dynamic geometry and coding environments.
nodes.bodyHtml: Dynamic tools let learners vary constraints rapidly and see invariants, but require prompts that focus reasoning.

nodes.label: Teacher questioning & prompts
nodes.summary: Ask questions that move learners up, down or sideways in the lattice.
nodes.bodyHtml: Questions such as “What stays the same?”, “Can you draw it?”, “What if this changes?” and “Why must it happen?” build agility.

nodes.label: Assessment for mathematical creativity
nodes.summary: Assess strategy choice, representation, conjecture, explanation, transfer and revision.
nodes.bodyHtml: Assessment should recognise mathematical movement, not just final answers.

nodes.label: Learning journals & visible traces
nodes.summary: Record conjectures, diagrams, examples, errors, revisions and reflections.
nodes.bodyHtml: Visible traces give learners material to revisit and reorganise; they make the learning journey inspectable.

nodes.label: Classroom culture for risk and rigour
nodes.summary: Normalise uncertainty, respectful challenge, evidence and revision.
nodes.bodyHtml: Culture determines whether learners dare to propose, test and defend ideas.

nodes.label: Indicators of mathematical agility
nodes.summary: Look for flexible movement between levels, representations and strategies.
nodes.bodyHtml: Evidence includes choosing a representation, inventing an example, spotting invariance, finding a counterexample and explaining transfer.

nodes.label: Curriculum mapping & progression
nodes.summary: Map how constraints develop across years: unit, equality, equivalence, function, proof, uncertainty.
nodes.bodyHtml: Progression is not only harder content; it is increasing agility across abstraction levels.

nodes.label: Plan–Try–Notice–Revise learning cycle
nodes.summary: Use iterative improvement for lessons, tasks and learner strategies.
nodes.bodyHtml: Teachers and learners use evidence from activity to redesign prompts, examples and representations.

nodes.label: Learning evidence beyond scores
nodes.summary: Use leading and lagging indicators: participation, explanation quality, representation range, fluency and transfer.
nodes.bodyHtml: Scores matter, but they must be interpreted alongside richer evidence of mathematical agency and understanding.

nodes.label: Lesson study & task refinement
nodes.summary: Collaboratively observe how learners respond to constraints, then refine the task.
nodes.bodyHtml: Lesson study treats the task as a design object and learner responses as evidence about constraints.

nodes.label: Teacher mathematical-pedagogical fluency
nodes.summary: Teachers need subject knowledge, representational fluency and sensitivity to learner reasoning.
nodes.bodyHtml: Creative teaching is not improvisation alone; it depends on deep knowledge of likely routes, misconceptions and extensions.

nodes.label: Learning loops & continuous curriculum improvement
nodes.summary: Convert learner explanations, errors and questions into improved sequences, examples and supports.
nodes.bodyHtml: The curriculum learns when teachers systematically feed classroom evidence back into design.

nodes.label: Challenge: Procedure lock-in
nodes.summary: Learners can perform steps but cannot explain, adapt or transfer them.
nodes.bodyHtml: Procedure lock-in occurs when the classroom rewards compliance with method over mathematical sense-making.

nodes.label: Pathology: Answer hunting
nodes.summary: Learners search for the expected answer rather than exploring constraints and reasons.
nodes.bodyHtml: Answer hunting collapses the lattice to one endpoint: speed, correctness and teacher approval.

nodes.label: Pathology: False pattern confidence
nodes.summary: Learners overgeneralise from too few examples and ignore counterexamples.
nodes.bodyHtml: Creative exploration needs constraint-testing, otherwise attractive patterns become fragile myths.

nodes.label: Case: Fraction art studio
nodes.summary: Learners create visual designs with equivalent fractions, area models and ratio constraints.
nodes.bodyHtml: Children design tiled artworks where colour proportions must match target fractions, then justify equivalence using diagrams and numbers.

nodes.label: Case: Playground geometry mapping
nodes.summary: Learners map routes, angles, symmetry and scale in a playground or local space.
nodes.bodyHtml: Movement through real space becomes a route into coordinates, turns, estimation, symmetry and scale drawing.

nodes.label: Case: Data story from class life
nodes.summary: Learners collect meaningful data about their routines, choices or environment and turn it into arguments.
nodes.bodyHtml: The class asks a real question, gathers data, visualises variation and debates what the evidence can and cannot show.

nodes.label: Case: “Use the method I showed you”
nodes.summary: A teacher accidentally suppresses mathematical agency by rejecting alternative strategies.
nodes.bodyHtml: The lesson recovers when alternative strategies are compared for efficiency, generality and explanation.

nodes.label: Case: Spreadsheet pattern machine
nodes.summary: Learners build a spreadsheet or coding model to vary rules and observe sequences, graphs and functions.
nodes.bodyHtml: Changing one formula cell becomes a way to experience variables, functions and generalisation dynamically.

nodes.label: Case: Song fluency without structure
nodes.summary: A class can chant multiplication facts but cannot use arrays, factors or scaling to solve unfamiliar problems.
nodes.bodyHtml: The remedy is to reconnect recall to arrays, area, grouping, ratio and flexible decomposition.

edges.why: This connection helps learners move from wonder before technique into quantity, measure & scale, keeping freedom tied to mathematical constraint.
Linking wonder before technique with mathematical feedback loops supports movement up and down the constraint lattice rather than isolated procedure.
wonder before technique provides the broader constraint; classroom culture for risk and rigour makes it teachable, visible or testable in mathematical activity.
When wonder before technique is neglected, concrete ↔ abstract movement often becomes mechanical; the link restores meaning, representation and judgement.
